topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sight hit me like a physical blow. Standing where the towering oak used to be, now just a jagged stump and splintered wood, my breath hitched in my throat. It had been thirty years since I’d put that sapling in the ground, a clumsy, hopeful gesture on a sun-drenched patch of earth behind the shed. My knuckles were white where I gripped the rusted garden trowel. I took a slow, unsteady step closer, the dry leaves crunching under my worn boots. "No," I whispered, the sound raspy and thin. "No, you can't just... erase that." A hot, tight pressure built behind my eyes, making the edges of my vision swim. I spun around, scanning the dense woods, searching for any sign—a discarded chainsaw, a truck, anything to explain this vandalism of my memory. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the tendons straining in my neck.

[story 2]

I walked back to the clearing, my pace quick and jerky, each footfall echoing the frantic drumming in my chest. The space where the tree had stood was desolate, a raw wound in the landscape. I stopped dead, my hands balling into fists so tight my nails dug crescents into my palms. A low, guttural sound escaped my chest, a sound entirely devoid of warmth. "This is not funny," I spat out, the words sharp and brittle. My shoulders tensed up to my ears, and I could feel the heat rising in my face, a deep flush spreading across my neck. I kicked at the base of the remaining trunk, the wood offering no resistance. Every muscle in my body vibrated with a furious energy, a desperate need to confront whoever had done this destruction.

[story 3]

It was a quiet afternoon, the kind where the air hangs heavy and still. Then, I saw it. The silhouette of the oak, reduced to broken limbs scattered across the muddy ground. A sudden, violent tremor ran through me, starting in my core and radiating outward. I dropped the gardening gloves; they landed silently in the damp soil. I didn't move for a long moment, just staring at the devastation. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously level, almost too calm. "You think this is acceptable?" I demanded, my gaze sweeping across the empty space, burning with a cold, hard fury. My breathing became shallow and rapid, a frantic attempt to keep the surging anger contained beneath the surface of my composure.
